chunk_summary: Guidelines for pediatric parenteral nutrition emphasize delayed initiation in stable children to reduce complications, while early PN is critical for neonates or high-risk cases. Nutrition risk screening tools like STRONGkids and PNST guide urgency and intervention planning.
learning_objectives: Understand the indications for early versus delayed parenteral nutrition in pediatric critical care.
Learn to apply nutrition risk screening tools such as STRONGkids and PNST in clinical practice.
Recognize the metabolic needs of neonates and high-risk pediatric patients requiring early parenteral nutrition.
Identify strategies to avoid iatrogenic overfeeding in critically ill children.
key_facts: Aggressive early PN in hemodynamically unstable children can increase infectious and metabolic complications; withholding PN for up to one week is reasonable if enteral progress is expected.
Early PN is indicated for neonates, especially very low birth weight infants or those with congenital gastrointestinal anomalies, when enteral feeding is not possible.
STRONGkids scoring: 0 (low risk), 1–3 (moderate risk), 4–5 (high risk); high scores should trigger dietitian involvement within 24 hours.
Acute-phase energy delivery should not exceed resting energy expenditure, estimated using the Schofield equation if indirect calorimetry is unavailable.
High-risk cases include severe wasting, oncology patients with negligible intake, or major postoperative ileus with no enteral tolerance expected for 5–7 days.
reference_quote: Early versus late parenteral nutrition in critically ill children (PEPaNIC trial) — withholding supplemental macronutrient PN for up to one week is reasonable in stable children to reduce complications.
document_key_topics: Timing decisions for parenteral nutrition in pediatric critical care
Nutrition risk screening tools and their application
Metabolic needs of neonates and high-risk pediatric patients
Strategies to prevent iatrogenic overfeeding in critically ill children
